text: Four trocars are visibly placed in the abdominal wall. The laparoscopic view shows the rectosigmoid mass and its attachment to the dome of the bladder.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to dissect the mesocolon, creating a mesenteric window. Both the inferior mesenteric artery and vein are isolated and clearly visible.
The surgeon ligates the isolated inferior mesenteric artery with two hemoclips and cuts it. Subsequently, the mesenteric vein is ligated with hemoclips and cut using an energy device.
The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral side, incising the white line of Toldt to mobilize the colon.
The surgeon includes the dome of the bladder, which has been invaded by cancer, in the specimen to ensure safe surgical margins during the resection.
The surgeon transects the rectum using surgical staplers. Subsequently, the superior wall of the bladder is repaired with a single layer continuous suture, ensuring the integrity of the bladder wall.

The surgeon performs colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, ensuring the connection between the colon and rectum. An air leak test is conducted, confirming the integrity of the anastomosis as negative for leaks.
